data.title: Allspring Government Securities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Strategy

  • Seeks to outperform the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Ex-Credit Index by investing in high-quality fixed-income instruments while maintaining a target duration of +/- one year of the benchmark
  • Typically holds most of its exposure in mortgage-backed securities, including collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs), and asset-backed securities issued or guaranteed by U.S. government agencies or government-sponsored enterprises
  • Seeks to invest predominantly in securities rated AAA by Standard & Poor's or an equivalent quality rating from another Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization

Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate ex Credit Bond Index, for the three-month period that ended September 30, 2025.
  • Duration and curve positioning were the largest contributors to performance, while sector and issue selection detracted. Quality had a neutral impact on performance for the quarter.

Market review

The U.S. economy sustained its momentum from the second quarter into the third quarter of 2025, with current activity indicators pointing to a second straight quarter of above-trend growth. Although second-quarter gross domestic product was revised upward due to stronger consumer spending, the first half of the year still posted below-trend growth of 1.6% annualized, weighed down by rapid and chaotic policy shifts. Growth in final sales to private domestic purchasers remained robust, supported by solid consumer and corporate fundamentals. Overall, economic strength continued to be driven by high-income households and large-cap corporations that remained less sensitive to interest rate changes than prior cycles.

Inflation edged higher in the third quarter, leaving year-over-year price growth modestly elevated around 3.0%. Goods inflation was driven by tariff-related price pass-through, while services excluding shelter inflation remained persistently high. Importantly, this worsening inflation trend occurred alongside a cooling labor market. Job growth slowed significantly, with nonfarm payrolls averaging roughly 29,000 over the past three months. The unemployment rate rose slightly to 4.3%, leaving the labor market tight. This was largely due to declining labor supply due to easing net immigration and an aging workforce.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered the policy rate by 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) to a range of 4.00–4.25% in the third quarter, citing a growing balance of risks between its two mandates. Central banks in other developed markets continued their easing cycles, with most approaching their respective neutral rate levels. Geopolitical tensions remained elevated.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.59%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.48%. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning

Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—contributor

  • The portfolio maintained a modest long duration positioning relative to the benchmark for most of the period as a hedge against macroeconomic and policy uncertainty, which contributed to performance. We moved to a flat duration posture in mid-September given we see near-term upside risks to yields. We maintained a curve-steepening bias throughout the second quarter expressed as an overweight in 2- and 5-year maturities and an underweight to the longer end. The portfolio benefited as rates fell across the curve during the quarter.
  • The Fed resumed rate cuts in the period amid growing internal divisions over the prioritization of its dual mandate—price stability versus full employment—raising questions about the appropriate pace of further easing. Fed funds ended the period in a range between 4.00% and 4.25%. The U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened during the period.
  • We expect credit conditions to remain healthy. The current outlook suggests one or two more rate cuts in 2025. We are carefully watching macroeconomics data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves.

Quality—neutral

  • Given the fund’s high-quality mandate, credit-quality allocation rarely will be a contributor or detractor versus the benchmark.

Sector—detractor

  • An overweight to securitized sectors detracted from performance during the quarter. The fund’s 1.5% allocation to non-agency collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) was the primary detractor as spreads on deeper discount bonds widened over the period. A 9% overweight to agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) contributed to performance as spreads tightened 5–10 bps.
  • The fund’s overall securitized exposure was close to unchanged over the quarter, with a 0.3% reduction in non-agency CMOs offset by an increase to agency MBS. The fund ended the third quarter with overweights to agency MBS and commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) of 9% and 13%, respectively. The fund’s exposure to out-of-benchmark CMOs was approximately 7%.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Issue selection detracted from performance overall. An overweight to GNMAs detracted, partially offset by an underweight to 15-years and selection within specified pools, which contributed. Positioning within the government sector, CMBS, and asset-backed securities were approximately neutral to performance.
  • Within MBS, we increased the fund’s overweight to higher coupons and reduced an overweight to discount coupons as premium coupons underperformed over the period.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

BENCHMARK DESCRIPTIONS:

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate ex Credit Index is composed of the Bloomberg U.S. Government Bond Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities Index and includes Treasury issues, agency issues, and mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index is an unmanaged index of prices of U.S. Treasury bonds with maturities of 1 to 30 years. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Mortgage Backed Securities (MBS) Index tracks fixed-rate agency mortgage backed pass-through securities guaranteed by Ginnie Mae (GNMA), Fannie Mae (FNMA), and Freddie Mac (FHLMC). The index is constructed by grouping individual to-be-announced-deliverable MBS pools into aggregates or generics based on program, coupon, and vintage. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody’s rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody’s rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMIG-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund’s duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond’s (or fund’s) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: All investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Securities issued by U.S. government agencies or government-sponsored entities may not be guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury. Consult the fund’s prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled intermediate government bond funds, the Government Securities Fund received 2 stars among 212 funds, 3 stars among 203 funds, and 3 stars among 169 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund’s total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Blackstone: I Still Can't Stomach The Valuation
data.description:
Near the end of August, I upgraded Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) from a sell to a hold rating. While the fundamentals showed that the firm was indeed best of breed, the valuation was a bit rich and so I decided to stay neutral on the stock. As you can see in the rating history chart below, Blackstone has experienced significant losses in recent months and today we'll examine their most recent results to see if this is a buying opportunity.

In the analysis below, it is shown that Blackstone's fundamentals remain very solid. Inflows demonstrate client confidence, strong investment performance indicate a good strategy, and robust bottom line results show that profitability is also solid. Like in my previous article, I am impressed by the firm. However, also like in my previous article, the valuation gap between Blackstone and competitors seems too large to be justified. As a result, at this time I have decided to maintain my hold rating despite the firm being very well run.

Inflows Show Healthy Client Activity

Before discussing the financial performance of the firm, let's take a look at their AUM and net flows to see how client activity is holding up. Investment performance can vary quarter to quarter but whether or not Blackstone is attracting ever more capital is a good indication of how clients perceive the firm. As shown above, total AUM hit $1.2417 trillion, up 12% with $54.2 billion of that increase coming from inflows. In the previous quarter, inflows were reported to be $52.1 billion while in the prior year period, it was just $40.5 billion. Therefore, as a whole, it seems clients are increasingly holding a favorable view on Blackstone's capabilities.

For their fee-earning AUM, they reported a 10% increase YoY to $906.2 billion but inflows of $39 billion were slightly less than the $41.9 billion we saw in Q2. Do note that in 2024 Q3, there were inflows of just $34.1 billion and so I would say that investors still don't have too much to complain about. The trustworthiness of the firm in the eyes of clients is shown to be rock solid through these inflow statistics.

Perpetual capital is really a key metric for Blackstone as the ability to invest in assets for the long run is core factor in unlocking value. As you can see above, they have hit $500.6 billion in total perpetual capital AUM and that represents a growth rate of 15%. In Q2, growth was reported to be 16% but still this Q3 increase outpaces the firm's total AUM growth rate. Therefore, I believe this indicates that Blackstone's long term focus is resonating with clients and in the long term, that should drive both client and shareholder value.

Investment Performance Is Solid

As stated above, investment performance can fluctuate and may not be the greatest indication of the long term success of a firm like Blackstone that's in for the long haul. However, Q3 figures show that the firm's investment strategy is likely working out. Apart from Real Estate, Blackstone's investment performance was very respectable and so you can see that there was broad strength. For real estate, they experienced depreciation in opportunistic both for Q3 and the LTM. Therefore, it seems commercial real estate remains weak relative to other asset classes. Again, they are in for the long haul and so the firm likely sees a recovery down the road.

Ready To Pounce

Blackstone's dry powder is up significantly from the previous quarter's $181.2 billion and the prior year period's $171.6 billion and so it seems that the firm is well-positioned to pounce on opportunities ahead. When comparing the pie chart on the right to the one for Q2, there are some notable differences. Firstly, dry powder for multi-asset investing and for private equity declined very slightly. Dry powder for private equity is actually very strong as they deployed $5.564 billion in Q3. For real estate, there was a $2.5 billion QoQ drop in dry powder after a $3.6 billion deployment in the quarter. Therefore, overall, it seems that clients aren't overly encouraged by what they see in that asset class. That's isn't too surprising given the performance figures shown earlier.

The most impressive dry powder metric is the $9.6 billion QoQ increase for credit & insurance. In Q3 alone, Blackstone deployed $16.656 billion in this asset class and so the considerable increase in dry powder indicates great interest. At this point, it seems that private credit is booming. Overall, the strength in dry powder indicates that the firm is ready to pounce but it is clear that investors are steering clear of real estate in relative terms.

Business Is Good

Finally, we arrive at the bottom line and results indicate a very profitable business. In fee-related terms, there was a 26% increase YoY in earnings as performance revenues soared 72%. Clearly, the successful execution of their investment strategy is powering growth for the firm.

For net realizations, there was an increase of 124%. As you can see above, while the realized performance compensation increase of 92% may seem a bit concerning to investors, note that realized performance revenues increased by an even greater margin of 117%. Therefore, it seems that profitability is solid.

At the end of the day, DE per common share was reported to be $1.52, up 50% YoY which is quite an acceleration from Q2's 26% increase. This figure also beat expectations by a rather impressive margin of $0.29 and so Q3 was overall a robust quarter for Blackstone in terms of their earnings.

Valuation Still Expensive

In my previous article, I compared Blackstone's P/B ratio to competitors KKR (KKR) and Apollo (APO) and concluded that the valuation gap was likely excessive. As you can see above, while the P/B ratio has contracted for all three companies, the valuation deviation between Blackstone and competitors has closed somewhat since late August. Blackstone is undoubtedly the leader in alternative assets but I still believe the valuation is somewhat rich.

Currently, Seeking Alpha rates Blackstone's growth as a B+ and their profitability as an A- relative to the Financials sector. While that is impressive note that KKR is rated as a B for growth and an A- for profitability. Therefore, while Blackstone's fundamentals still have the edge, do they really justify a P/B premium of around ~237%? I don't believe so and that's why I view the valuation as still a bit too expensive for an entry right now. As of this writing, Seeking Alpha rates Blackstone's valuation as an F overall, confirming my evaluation.

Technical Analysis

In terms of the technicals, there are some mixed signals but overall, there are some good signs for the bulls. As you can see, the 50 day SMA is falling towards the 200 day SMA and so the dreaded death cross may happen in the not so distant future. However, there are some bottoming signs.

Firstly, the 9 day EMA is closing the gap with the 21 day EMA as a signal that near term bearish momentum is receding. Secondly, the stock has reclaimed the Bollinger Bands midline on the current trading day as another indication that the bears may now be in retreat. The MACD and RSI also indicate that there may be bullish reversal potential as both showed positive divergence at the recent November low. You can see that both indicators remained above their lows of earlier this month. Furthermore, while the RSI has yet to regain 50, the MACD recently had a significant bullish crossover. Overall, while a death cross may be unavoidable, there are many other signals that indicate better days are ahead for the stock.

Takeaway

It's not a bad idea to buy a best of breed business at a fair valuation but is the premium over KKR really fair? Blackstone has the stronger fundamentals (growth & profitability) overall according to Seeking Alpha and is the leader in this space but the nearly 240% P/B ratio premium is simply too much to stomach for me personally. The stock is seeing bullish reversal potential as shown above and this elevated valuation relative to peers may continue as investors perceive the firm to be the best in the business. To be more conservative however, I have decided to maintain my hold rating on Blackstone despite being thoroughly impressed by their fundamental robustness.

data.title: American Century Short Duration Strategic Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Bonds advanced for the quarter. U.S. investment-grade bonds logged their third straight quarterly gain. Labor market weakness and growing expectations for the Federal Reserve (Fed) to cut interest rates largely supported the bond market’s positive performance.

The economy bounced back. After contracting in the first quarter, the U.S. economy rallied back in the second quarter with a 3.8% annualized gain. Data released in the third quarter showed the manufacturing and services sectors expanded, and retail sales were robust, but the labor market weakened. The government overstated job growth for the 12 months ended in March by nearly 1 million jobs.

The Fed cut rates. The Fed cut rates by 0.25% in September, marking the first easing move since December. The target lending rate range dropped to 4% to 4.25%. Amid signs of a weakening job market, policymakers indicated rising unemployment represents a bigger economic threat than stubborn inflation. Most officials penciled in another 0.5% of easing by year-end.

Treasury yields declined modestly. Amid resilient growth and persistent inflation, Treasury yields were volatile but ended the quarter lower overall. Labor market woes and growing expectations for Fed easing generally drove the decline. The two-year Treasury yield declined 0.1% to 3.62%, while the 10-year yield fell 0.08% to 4.15%. The yield curve between two and 10 years steepened slightly.

Inflation data were mixed. The annual headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose from 2.7% in July to 2.9% in August, its highest since January. Core CPI remained unchanged at 3.1%, and the annual core personal consumption expenditures price index, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, held steady at 2.9%, well above the Fed’s 2% target. Inflation break-even rates rose modestly, and Treasury inflation-protected securities outperformed nominal Treasuries.

All sectors posted gains. Treasury yields declined, credit spreads tightened, and all investment-grade bond sectors advanced in the quarter, led by the outperforming corporate bond and mortgage-backed securities sectors. Treasuries advanced but lagged bonds. High-yield corporates slightly lagged investment-grade corporates.

Portfolio Performance Review

Sector allocations drove results. An out-of-index position in high-yield credit was a main driver of the portfolio’s outperformance compared with the benchmark. Positions in banking and media and entertainment were top contributors. An overweight relative to the benchmark to the securitized sector also contributed. Our preference for out-of-index credit-sensitive securitized securities lifted performance.

Security selection aided performance. Security selection among investment-grade corporates was also a key driver of performance compared with the benchmark. Our selections in the financials sector, primarily banks, offered notable results. We lifted exposure to the sector, favoring higher-quality, higher-yielding securities. Meanwhile, our selections among emerging markets bonds slightly detracted.

Yield curve strategy contributed. Yield curve exposure modestly contributed to results compared with the benchmark, largely due to duration positioning. Pricing dynamics in the short-term Treasury market prompted us to reduce duration, but the portfolio’s duration remained longer than the index’s. Carry effects also boosted performance.

Portfolio Positioning

The economy may stall. We believe below-trend growth(annualized gross domestic product of less than 1.5%) may unfold, as the cumulative effects of restrictive monetary policy and tariff uncertainties weigh on the economy. Lower labor supply and waning demand should generate a labor market in which hiring and firing remain relatively low. Growth should improve next year amid fiscal and monetary support.

Credit metrics remain solid. In our view, the corporate credit backdrop remains upbeat. Fundamentals are sound, we feel yields are attractive, and defaults are low. This should fuel robust mergers and acquisitions activity, which could have mixed implications for the market. Additionally, we will closely monitor the effects of tariffs on corporate profitability and margins, consumer spending and risk appetites.

Searching for themes. With valuations near historically tight levels, we continue to focus on themes to uncover value. Specifically, we look for bonds offering spread tightening potential due to the issuers’ exposure to positive events or catalysts. Our stringent credit research helps uncover these securities. As always, we will remain nimble, highly selective and focused on valuations.

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities (ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points (BPS): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower- yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for “carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index (CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration (CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or “cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest- paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures (usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation (GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield (HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade (IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard& Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A “market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships(MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher- yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities ((MBS)): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing (QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later (to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities (maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality (credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard “swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills(maturing in one year or less), notes(maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

data.title: John Hancock Disciplined Value Mid Cap Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

U.S. equities performed well in the quarter, reflecting the favorable backdrop of steady global growth, positive corporate earnings trends, and waning concerns about tariffs. Additionally, the U.S. Federal Reserve’s decision to cut interest rates by a quarter point in September fueled expectations that further easing could be in store before year end. Mid-cap value stocks comfortably outpaced their growth counterparts, which represented a notable contrast to the prevailing trend in the large-cap space.

We expect volatility will remain elevated in the coming months. Tariff news in the United States, conflicts overseas, and uncertainty regarding the degree of central banks’ ability to lower rates could all affect investor sentiment. Despite these potential headwinds, we continue to identify ample opportunities for bottom-up security selection.

Contributors and detractors

Stock selection in the IT and financials sectors detracted from relative performance. In the former, the fund was hurt by its zero weightings in a number of AI-related stocks that outperformed, as well as positions in Check Point Software Technologies Ltd. (CHKP) and Microchip Technology, Inc. (MCHP). However, the tech sector was also home to two of the fund’s top individual contributors: Seagate Technology Holdings plc (STX) and InterDigital, Inc. (IDCC). The stocks benefited from anticipation of rising storage demand and better-than-expected profits, respectively. In financials, the wealth manager LPL Financial Holdings, Inc. (LPLA) lagged due to the combination of lower interest rates, asset inflows that were somewhat lighter than expected, and some challenges in integrating a recent acquisition. Ameriprise Financial, Inc. (AMP) also hurt results, as did the lack of a position in Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD).

On the positive side, selection in the industrials sector contributed. Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI) announced it was spinning out its distribution business and resolving its long-term asbestos-related liability with Honeywell (HON), removing an overhang from the stock. In addition, Resideo beat earnings estimates and raised its forward guidance. Consumer discretionary was another area of strength for the fund, highlighted by positions in Somnigroup International, Inc. (SGI) (formerly Tempur Sealy), eBay, Inc. (EBAY), and AutoZone, Inc. (AZO).

Allocation was a further plus, led by overweights in the outperforming consumer discretionary and information technology sectors.

Portfolio changes

We continued to increase the fund’s weighting in healthcare. We added seven positions in the sector over the past six months, indicating the growing number of opportunities we’ve found in this area. Consumer discretionary is still the fund’s leading overweight due in part to the additions we made following the tariff-driven sell-off of the second quarter. More recently, we established new positions in the homebuilder D.R. Horton, Inc. and the retailer Williams-Sonoma, Inc. Utilities and communication services were the largest underweights. One notable shift was that Howmet Aerospace, Inc. dropped out of the portfolio’s top 10 holdings, as we trimmed the position after the stock’s strong recent performance.

The average market capitalization of the fund’s holdings was below that of the index at the close of the period, which is a departure from our historical positioning. The portfolio’s valuation was roughly in line with the benchmark, but it had a greater free cash flow yield and higher return on equity.

data.title: Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Emerging markets equity strategy seeks to maximize total return and generate higher income than the reference Index, using model-driven stock selection call options writing.

Key takeaways

  • During the quarter, the Fund detracted from the performance primarily due to the higher dividend yield. Conversely, the core model contributed to performance, where four out of five pillars contributed.
  • Inflation is easing, labor markets are softening, and the U.S. Federal Reserve has pivoted to rate cuts. Despite tariff pressures, consumer sensitivity and constrained spending should limit inflation persistence. U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth is projected to remain positive but moderate, with large-cap equities favored due to strong fundamental factors and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven growth potential.
  • Global momentum is mixed. Europe and the United Kingdom face fiscal and structural headwinds, while Japan shows promise amid reforms. China struggles with property sector stress and cautious consumers, though offshore equities benefit from technology exposure. International bonds offer less attractive yields, and emerging markets (EM) debt faces uneven fundamentals.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Fund provided a total return of 8.45% on a net asset value basis, and a total return of 8.50% on a market price basis. The Fund's reference Index, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, returned 10.64%.

Equity Portfolio

The equity sleeve underperformed the reference Index, primarily due to the higher dividend yield. Conversely, the core model contributed to performance, where four out of five pillars contributed.

On the sector level, selection in the information technology, consumer discretionary, and health care sectors detracted. Conversely, stock selection within the financials, energy, and industrials sectors contributed.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) carefully before investing. For a free copy of the Fund's prospectus or summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

Regionally, Asia Ex-Japan detracted from performance. Conversely, Africa & Middle East, Latin America, and Europe-Non-Euro regions contributed to performance.

At the individual stock level, the largest detractors were an underweight position in Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA), overweighted in HCL Technologies Ltd., and did not own Baidu, Inc. Class A (BIDU). Conversely, the key contributors were an underweight position in HDFC Bank Ltd. (HDB) and the overweight positions in Delta Electronics, Inc. and CMOC Group Ltd. (OTCPK:CMCLF) Class H.

Option Portfolio

The Fund's covered call strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. This strategy had a negative impact on the returns during the period. The Fund typically implements this strategy by writing call options on the EM exchange- traded fund (ETF). The strike prices of the options written are usually at or near the money, with expiration dates around one month at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

EM stocks performed well during the quarter. China's rebound in technology and policy support drove much of the rally, while Taiwan and Korea added momentum via strong exports. Some markets, like India, lagged amid trade pressures and weaker investor sentiment. The Fed's rate cut helped lift risk appetite and pushed capital toward higher-growth emerging regions. The strength of EM could continue if global interest rates stay low and demand for technology and raw materials holds up; stable government policy in China and consistent consumer spending worldwide will be key to sustaining momentum.

data.title: Credo Technology: AI Momentum Vs. Overvaluation (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Credo Technologies (CRDO) has kept soaring since early July 2025 despite my previous bearish call that cited significant overvaluation. It was a strong 70% rally, partially fueled by AI-related FOMO but also due to aggressive upward EPS revisions over the last few months. I have to give credit to the management because their track record of capitalizing on the data center boom is impressive. The AI momentum is also strong with a few new large AI infrastructure projects announced just recently. However, valuation analysis says that expectations are too high. I think that CRDO is likely to deliver strong earnings next week. However, this earnings season showed us that even Nvidia did not rally after its stellar past quarter's performance. I do not want to bet against the AI momentum again, meaning that waiting on the sidelines with a "Hold" rating is a prudent approach.

FQ2 2026 earnings preview

Credo releases its FQ2 2026 earnings on December 1st. The company does not have a very long earnings surprise record, but even with a relatively small sample there are some useful takeaways. The management is usually quite conservative in the EPS estimates because six out of eight last earnings releases were marked with 25%+ positive EPS surprises. Revenue surprises were also mostly wide over the same period. A company that is able to deliver large EPS and revenue surprises usually deserves strong investors' confidence, which is always good before the earnings release.

Besides investors' confidence, CRDO also has confidence of Wall Street analysts before the upcoming earnings release. Earnings revisions were all double-digit upgrades over the last three months, which is not only for FQ2 2026 but for several quarters ahead as well. The most impressive part is that CRDO's EPS is expected to soar by triple-digit percentages for two quarters in a row. The fact that CRDO's revenue and EPS are expected to maintain double-digit growth over the next several quarters indicates Wall Street's confidence in the company's ability to capitalize on the data center boom.

And I have to say that Wall Street's confidence in Credo's ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds is not from thin air. CRDO has been absolutely brilliant in driving revenue growth over the last four quarters by accelerating YoY growth from 64% in FQ2 2025 to staggering 274% in FQ1 2026. The best part for investors here is that it was not just growth at all costs but profitable growth. The operating leverage is certainly there with the operating margin improving from -11.7% to 27.2% between aforementioned quarters.

Furthermore, despite revenue soaring by 64% YoY in FQ2 2025, for the upcoming earnings release a 226% YoY revenue growth is projected. It is impressive because usually it is difficult to accelerate growth that much when the comparative number spiked already. As we know from the previous earnings call, Credo's ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds is mostly due to the active cable [AEC] product line, which is in high demand for data centers. The recent licensing agreement with the Siemon Company regarding AEC patents looks promising because it solidifies the value and proprietary nature of this technology. Among the positives for CRDO are also broader market recognition and potential for increased market penetration. With these favorable factors emerging as a result of the licensing agreement deal, I think that Credo's ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds has strengthened.

Industry-wise, the last few days confirmed that the most notable AI players are still aggressive in their AI infrastructure expansion plans. For example, Amazon considers investing another $15 billion in a new data center related project in Northern Indiana. Elon Musk's xAI is expected to close the new $15 billion funding round soon with the proceeds expected to be allocated in AI hardware as well. Developer of another popular LLM, Anthropic, also has ambitious plans to increase its computing capacity in the foreseeable future. Therefore, AI tailwinds are still strong, and I expect it to positively affect CRDO's revenue guidance for the full FY2026.

Besides headline numbers, I would also recommend investors to pay attention to key profitability ratios dynamic. As I mentioned before, gross and operating margins were expanding quite aggressively over the last few quarters. If CRDO continues demonstrating expansion of these metrics in FQ2 2026, it will highly likely add more confidence to investors. However, if there are signs that margins are not expanding or expanding slowly, it might be a warning sign indicating that CRDO's business model achieved its full operating leverage potential. Margin dynamic is also crucial in terms of Credo's cash flow generating capacity because any business faces a moment when organic growth slows down and having strong cash generating capacity is crucial to accumulate strong financial position that can be potentially utilized for prospective M&A.

Overvaluation offsets fundamental brilliance

CRDO rallied by around 230% over the last twelve months and by 130% year-to-date. CRDO bulls enjoy strong momentum, which is always a bullish factor. However, there is always a very subtle line between healthy optimism and overly high expectations. When I look at Credo's valuation ratios, I think that we are more in the "overly high expectations" area.

Of course, with CRDO's aggressive revenue and EPS growth, high TTM and 1Y forward multiples are perfectly fine. Since the calendar Q3 earnings season revealed that tech giants are willing to invest more in AI infrastructure during calendar year 2026, CRDO's elevated FY2027 forward P/E ratio estimate of 58 is also fine. However, the EPS growth rate is expected to moderate as the rule of large numbers will start working against Credo given the numbers were soaring over the last several quarters. It is just the basic math suggesting that it is far easier to deliver EPS growth when the comparative EPS is just a few cents [like in FY2024]. But because quarterly EPS numbers are expected to grow aggressively over the next few quarters, the comparatives will grow around tenfold in FY2027 compared to FY2024 levels.

Furthermore, financial strength of tech giants is not infinite and it is highly uncertain whether they will continue ramping up data center investments after calendar 2026. I think that the forward FY2028 P/E ratio estimate of 46 reflects that data center buildout will continue growing aggressively dollar-wise, which is an extremely risky bet. These expectations are highly likely mostly linked to optimistic expectations around the global data center spending spree because CRDO is a pure AI play at the moment as the company's business mix is mostly concentrated in generating revenues from data centers. Thus, CRDO's growth potential significantly depends on the strength of data center tailwinds.

Finally, last week Nvidia released stellar Q3 earnings with wide revenue and EPS beats and quite aggressive guidance. Nevertheless, the market cap of the world's largest company declined slightly over the last week. That might indicate that AI-related optimism is near peak levels at the moment.

Factors against upgrading

Actually, given Nvidia's weak share price performance after the earnings release might mean that I should better stick to the bearish rating. I am upgrading rating to a "Hold" because CRDO's revenue growth keeps accelerating aggressively. However, there is no guarantee that Mr. Market expects Credo's revenue to accelerate even faster. Moreover, with extremely high valuation ratios like CRDO has, even a double-beat against consensus estimates does not guarantee that the stock will avoid post-earnings sell-off.

The post-earnings sell-off risks are also high because of the massive rally CRDO delivered over last twelve months. A 230%+ capital gain for the ones who bought last November might be too tempting to miss out such opportunity to book large profits.

Moreover, insiders have been selling quite actively in October and November, which is another warning sign before the upcoming earnings release. Active insider selling is particularly warning in CRDO's case because it is not a company that has aggressive stock-based compensation. which means that insiders are not getting many shares as remuneration.

Bottom line

To conclude, I am upgrading CRDO to a "Hold". Betting against accelerating revenue growth amid AI momentum was a big mistake last time. The company's fundamentals are strong and the ability to capitalize on data center boom as well. However, I think that aggressive insider selling and generous valuation offset all the positives.

data.title: Kroger: Undervalued Retail Giant Positioned To Benefit From Macro Tailwinds
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

The Kroger Co. (KR) is one of the largest grocery retailers in the US, with a significant store footprint and well-known private label brands. With the company developing well despite the competitive pressure and overall weak environment due to high interest rates and their implications, I believe KR still offers a buying opportunity especially as rate cuts and long-term tailwinds advance despite near-term headwinds.

Kroger has over 2,700 stores under various names across the US, with advantages coming from their significant data collection, strong private label brands and a growing e-commerce business complementing their new store growth.

During their latest report, KR reported a $1.72 billion free cash flow in H1’25, and thanks to the favorable changes in working capital and slightly lower CAPEX, that’s significantly better than the ~$1.29 billion reported in H1’24. As a note, this was achieved on roughly the same sales and net earnings.

In fact, this H1 FCF is already similar to their FCF from the entire 2024, which reached $1.78 billion, while 2023 recorded $2.88 billion and $1.42 billion in 2022, although we should also note the high jump in CAPEX seen from 2023.

KR updated their FY25 guidance during the latest report, expecting lower sales growth, operating profit and EPS, but maintaining the FCF and CAPEX expectations at $2.8 billion to $3 billion and $3.6 billion to $3.8 billion, respectively. This would be a decent drop in CAPEX - in line with their cost-cutting approach that I’ll mention below - while the midpoint $2.9 billion FCF would be solid for the $43.78 billion market cap, placing them at a P/FCF ratio of around 15.

Financially, based on KR’s latest report, we see an okay financial position, with the current assets slightly below the current liabilities but a significant (and growing) amount of cash and cash equivalents that can offer some flexibility. The company’s debt of ~$18 billion (current plus long-term) remains manageable, with 1.70% to 8.00% Senior Notes due all the way through 2064.

Dividends & Buybacks

KR pays a ~2.12% dividend yield, raising it by 9% in Q2, or about $885 million over the past 12 months, but their buybacks are much more significant. Shortly after their $7.5 billion authorization from about a year ago, they entered into a $5 billion accelerated buyback agreement with Wells Fargo Bank, National Association and Citibank, which by itself represented a ~11.42% yield based on today’s price.

Risks & Opportunities

One big risk to keep in mind will come from the competition. This industry is already subject to very thin margins by default - which doesn’t make it bad, but increases the risk - and we’re looking at more and more competition from all around the globe.

Not only do they have the other powerhouses in the industry such as Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT) or even Amazon (AMZN) with Whole Foods, but we’re looking at foreign stores also aiming to ramp up their US footprint, with companies like Aldi, Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY) behind Food Lion or Schwarz Group behind Lidl - the latter being Europe’s largest retailer, with ~18% market share in Europe’s grocery market and nearly twice the revenue of the second competitor.

If either of these (especially Lidl) is allowed by the local giants to get anything close to what they are in Europe and fully develop their logistics and private label supply chains, they can be very hard to beat. It may not be a big issue yet for something like Walmart thanks to their size and ability/liquidity to respond to this, but Kroger is a bit smaller than Lidl’s parent in terms of sales (see source in the paragraph above), and these stores have been doing great especially during harder times thanks to their cheap private label options (considered “deep discount” stores by some), so it’s hard to know how the dynamics will shift if they want to accelerate their growth in the US the same way they did in Europe - with some cities having a Lidl nearby almost wherever you are, similar to Kroger.

Still, Kroger continued to be a top grocer in terms of growth last year however, although Aldi and Publix beat them in terms of both store count and square feet added in 2024.

In fact, Kroger and Lidl had a dispute before regarding Kroger’s “Private Selection” and Lidl’s “Preferred Selection,” with KR filing a lawsuit in 2017 (almost immediately after Lidl entered the US market) where they claim that this is an infringement that could cause irreparable damage. The lawsuit was shortly after dismissed permanently.

However, there are several studies showing Lidl’s or Aldi’s impact wherever they enter, forcing others to reduce prices and also causing what’s known as the “Lidl effect” or “Aldi effect” nowadays, pioneering the private-label market. Although Lidl isn’t as large in the US, Aldi is aggressively expanding and plans to become the second-largest grocer after Walmart, which is definitely no small feat especially since their aggressive expansion technically began in the 2010s after testing the markets, and is still going on.

One thing remains certain however, private labels are definitely a key thing to keep focusing on, continuing their significant growth to ~20.7% of total sales. This tailwind is definitely interesting, shifting away from the traditional marketing based on selling brands and pushing the brand power into the retailer’s hands rather than the producer’s. This is a plus for Kroger, with Kroger, Simple Truth and Private Selection being key for KR’s sales, representing ~27% of their overall sales and having a ~40% US household penetration - although it’s nowhere near the ~80% of sales for Aldi, which has a different business model that’s based on this.

To capitalize on this competition, Kroger can however try to expand through an acquisition, although their October 2022 attempt of acquiring Albertsons (ACI) for $24.6 billion, although it was denied as the FTC argued that this would risk reducing competition at the expense of both consumers and workers. To be fair, that can be said about most acquisitions ever, and I don’t think this would present a significant hit to the consumers or workers given their relatively small size in a giant market that’s, again, flourishing with competitors that include existing titans and new contenders.

It’s even worse that of all the recent notable M&A activity in the sector in 2024, this was the only one that was blocked, although it was also massive compared to most of these.

Albertsons sued Kroger shortly after, arguing that KR did not take the necessary steps and failed to take “any and all actions” to secure the regulatory approval - claiming that it breached their end of the agreement by “refusing to divest the necessary assets, ignoring feedback from regulators, rejecting divestiture buyers and failing to cooperate with Albertsons.”

The lawsuit advanced and Albertsons even said that Kroger had a “classic case of buyer’s remorse” after the negative reaction from the market/investors, while KR had its own arguments and allegations of a “surreptitious scheme” between Albertsons and C&S Wholesale Grocers. These lawsuits are still ongoing and can cost KR billions, so it’s a risk to remember.

Kroger’s CEO Rodney McMullen also resigned recently after a board investigation into his conduct (Albertsons also changed the CEO) finding that his personal conduct was “inconsistent” with certain company policies.

Another trend that KR is taking advantage of is the integration of AI, data collection and consumer analytics, with their e-commerce platform and partnerships with Instacart (CART), Uber Eats (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH) further supporting their online approach. KR plans to capitalize on their profitable e-commerce growth, aiming to increase profitability in the segment by ~$400 million that would then be reinvested back into the company through lower prices and better store conditions while also closing underperforming stores and firing people to re-allocate assets.

KR’s interim CEO mentioned during the latest Earnings Release that they still see several opportunities to reduce costs besides these (also planning to close 3 robotic warehouses in January 2026):

And as we are improving our store and e-commerce shopping experiences, we're also taking meaningful steps to reduce our cost structure. In the second quarter, we were pleased with our OG&A rate improvement, and we'll continue to aggressively look for ways to reduce costs throughout the company. We believe that many cost opportunities remain.

They also talked about the low estimated impact of tariffs thanks to their domestic operations during the same report, continuing to monitor their developments:

We continue to keep a close watch on the changing tariff environment. As a domestic food retailer, we expect a smaller impact than some of our competitors. We continue to be proactive to address exposure where we do have it, and our approach remains to raise prices as a last resort to ensure that we keep prices as low as possible for our customers. Tariffs have not had a material impact on our business thus far and as of now, do not expect them to going forward.

Another key opportunity to keep an eye on will be US rate cuts, as the company depends significantly on discretionary budgets. With higher rates, the Fed fights inflation by slowing down the economy, affecting discretionary budgets since variable (mortgage/credit/etc.) costs go up on top of the increase in prices (inflation) that often outpace salary growth. Meanwhile, rate cuts should do the opposite, while also lowering KR’s interest expenses as they refinance and allow them to expand with cheaper new debt.

Still, with the US Government recently going through its longest shutdown in history - even though it’s over now - we’ve seen some very important side effects for these cuts: the BLS halting its data collection and publishing, with a new release only in November showing mixed results, with stronger-than-expected non-farm payrolls in September and rising unemployment, but downward revisions for August and July. This delay caused quite significant drops in the broader market, as the Fed had less data and less time to process it before their early-December meeting, with Powell previously citing the uncertainty around inflation and unemployment during the October meeting.

The political environment is also something to keep in mind as potential risks, with SNAP benefits being affected by the shutdown (paying about 65% of usual benefits) and also targeted by the Trump administration, being in favor of expanding SNAP work requirements to more groups including parents of older children (14+), adults aged 55–64, veterans, and others. This is on top of his anti-immigration policies and an expectable anti-benefit politics, potentially hurting the demand for KR and the market as a whole - potentially boosting the demand for discount or deep discount retailers (note that KR is also working on lowering prices).

So, KR’s new management will have to navigate this rapidly-evolving environment even more carefully, with several near-term headwinds to monitor and several long-term tailwinds and trends to capitalize on.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse set of peers including Walmart, Target, Albertsons, Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM), Costco (COST) and Dollar General (DG), we see relatively attractive valuation metrics in almost everything. Looking at these, I could also see why they were interested in Albertsons - potentially delivering solid per share accretion - although the market didn’t like it for either of them (especially Albersons, who crashed over 20% on the day of the announcement).

Valuation

Starting with KR’s midpoint expectations of $2.9 billion in FCF, I’ll assume a 6.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (similar to the 5Y average Fwd. FCF per share growth rate of 6.34%), to account for rate cuts lowering their interest expenses and potentially boosting the growth combined with their cost savings, still ramped-up CAPEX and tailwinds, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a WACC of 7.5% (above my estimated ~6.75%; assumptions: ~7.2% cost of equity, ~4.6% cost of debt), with ~$20.3 billion in net debt and 665 million shares outstanding as of their latest report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $72.21 billion, an equity value of $51.91 billion and an intrinsic value of $78.06 per share.

Conclusions

I rate KR a Buy thanks to their solid fundamentals, strong position, growth potential and long-term tailwinds combined with their growth initiatives and cost-cutting program. Risks remain, especially as they are still undergoing the CEO search while the environment is actively changing, but they seem to be on the right track to keep benefitting from it in the long-term.
